sections.title: Common words
rows: after
I'll send you the results **after** we check everything.
as soon as
She'll call you **as soon as** she hears the news.
before
It might be a while **before** we get an answer.
until / till
I won't go to bed **until** I finish this chapter.
when
You'll feel better **when** you get some rest.
while
I'll wait here **while** you get changed.

sections.title: Be careful
items: ✓ **When** you get some rest, you'll feel better.

sections.title: Compare
rows: although + subject + verb, subject + verb
**Although** the film got bad reviews, I really enjoyed it.

sections.title: Be careful
items: ✓ I really enjoyed the film, **although** it got bad reviews.

sections.title: Compare
rows: in spite of / despite + -ing form, subject + verb
**In spite of** training hard, she didn't win the race.
in spite of / despite + noun, subject + verb
**Despite** the heavy traffic, we arrived on time.

sections.title: Be careful
items: ✓ She didn't win the race, **despite** training hard.

sections.title: Compare
rows: Subject + verb. However, subject + verb.
The plan seemed perfect. **However**, something went wrong.

sections.title: Be careful
items: ✓ The plan seemed perfect. Something went wrong, **however**.

exercises.title: A: Time clauses
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with one time word or phrase.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with one time word or phrase.

items.sentence: I'll phone you ...... the train leaves.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is action before another, so the correct answer is "before".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is wait up to a point, so the correct answer is "until".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: We'll go outside ...... the rain stops.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is when something happens, so the correct answer is "when".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: I'll tell you the answer ...... I find it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is immediately after, so the correct answer is "as soon as".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "as soon as", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Brush your teeth ...... you go to sleep.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is do something first, so the correct answer is "before".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: She reads a book ...... she waits for the bus.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is two actions at the same time, so the correct answer is "while".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

exercises.title: B: Although vs despite
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with although, despite or in spite of.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with although, despite or in spite of.

items.sentence: ...... it was very late, they kept studying.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is contrast with clause, so the correct answer is "Although".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: ...... feeling ill, he went to work.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is contrast + -ing, so the correct answer is "Despite".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: She enjoyed the trip ...... the bad weather.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is contrast + noun phrase, so the correct answer is "in spite of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "in spite of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: ...... his mistakes, he passed the driving test.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is contrast + noun, so the correct answer is "Despite".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: ...... the restaurant was expensive, we ate there.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is contrast clause, so the correct answer is "Although".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: ...... the cold, the children played outside.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is contrast + noun, so the correct answer is "In spite of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "In spite of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

exercises.title: C: Choose the connector
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct connector from the box.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with the correct connector from the box.

items.sentence: Don't turn off the computer ...... the update finishes.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is wait for completion, so the correct answer is "until".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: ...... she was scared, she went on stage.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is contrast clause, so the correct answer is "Although".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: ...... the long journey, we weren't tired.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is contrast + noun, so the correct answer is "Despite".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: You won't improve ...... you practise every day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is condition (if not), so the correct answer is "unless".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: The hotel was cheap. ......, it was very comfortable.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is contrast between sentences, so the correct answer is "However".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: I'll wash the dishes ...... you clean the table.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is simultaneous actions, so the correct answer is "while".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
